coredata.dc:title: Sea urchin-like asphalt-based graphene quantum dots/nickel cobalt bimetallic hydroxide for all-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yani
Xie, Lijing
Zhou, Yi
Li, Yongfeng
Chen, Cheng-Meng
coredata.dc:description: Composite materials combining carbon materials and transition metal hydroxides offer advantages such as high specific capacitance, high conductivity and low cost. However, the reported energy density of supercapacitors has been relatively low, limiting their widespread application. In this study, a high-performance hybrid electrode material, consisting of asphalt-based graphene quantum dots (AGQDs) and nickel-cobalt layered double hydroxide (NiCo-LDH) sea urchin-like structure, was successfully synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. By fully leveraging the cooperative interactions between NiCo-LDH and AGQDs, the electrode's charge transfer and storage capacity were significantly enhanced, demonstrating excellent electron transfer ability and electrochemical characteristics. The AGQDs/NiCo-LDH composite exhibited a specific capacitance of 1671.27 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, along with outstanding cyclic stability (retaining 75.94 % of capacitance after 5000 cycles). The assembled AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC Asymmetric supercapacitors (ASC) demonstrated a high energy density of 112.63 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750.83 W kg−1. This breakthrough establishes AGQDs as a leading contender for high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems, opening up new prospects for sustainable and efficient energy solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Asphalt-based graphene quantum dots
NiCo-LDH
All-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH.
Characterizations of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 (a) and (b) SEM images, (c) TEM image, (d) HRTEM and SAED images and (e) HAADF images.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (c) pore diameter distribution curve of NiCo-LDH-120, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-100-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-140-20.
The comparison of XPS survey spectra of (a) Ni 2p (b) Co 2p (c) O1s and (d) C 1s spectra for four samples of NiCo-LDH-120, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30.

(a, b) Comparative analysis of CV and GCD curves for different electrodes at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and a current density of 1 A g−1, respectively. (c, d) CV and GCD curves of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 electrode with varying scan rates and current densities. (e) Evaluation of specific capacitance for the electrodes at different current densities. (f) Nyquist plots with an inset illustrating the equivalent circuit diagram. (g) Cycling performance of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 electrode at a current density of 10 A g−1.

Electrochemical testing of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC ASC. (a) CV curves of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 and AC at 5 mV s−1. (b) CV curves with different potential windows at 50 mV s−1. (c) CV curves at scan rates ranging from 5 to 50 mV s−1. (d) GCD curves at current densities from 1 to 10 A g−1. (e) Cycling performance at the current density of 10 A g−1.

Electrochemical testing of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC all-solid-state ASCs device. (a) Schematic diagram of the ASC device. (b) CV curves with different potential windows at 50 mV s−1. (c) CV curves at scan rates ranging from 5 to 50 mV s−1. (d) GCD curves at current densities from 1 to 10 A g−1. (e) Rate performance. (f) Ragone plots of the ASC and previously reported ASCs. (g) Cycling performance at the current density of 10 A g−1 (The inset showed the LED device powered by two all-solid-state ASCs).
Comparison of current hydroxides with previously reported hydroxides.

head.ce:title: Sea urchin-like asphalt-based graphene quantum dots/nickel cobalt bimetallic hydroxide for all-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biomedical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biomedical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Sciences and Opto-Electronic Technology, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Sciences and Opto-Electronic Technology
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Sciences and Opto-Electronic Technology, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Composite materials combining carbon materials and transition metal hydroxides offer advantages such as high specific capacitance, high conductivity and low cost. However, the reported energy density of supercapacitors has been relatively low, limiting their widespread application. In this study, a high-performance hybrid electrode material, consisting of asphalt-based graphene quantum dots (AGQDs) and nickel-cobalt layered double hydroxide (NiCo-LDH) sea urchin-like structure, was successfully synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. By fully leveraging the cooperative interactions between NiCo-LDH and AGQDs, the electrode's charge transfer and storage capacity were significantly enhanced, demonstrating excellent electron transfer ability and electrochemical characteristics. The AGQDs/NiCo-LDH composite exhibited a specific capacitance of 1671.27 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, along with outstanding cyclic stability (retaining 75.94 % of capacitance after 5000 cycles). The assembled AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC Asymmetric supercapacitors (ASC) demonstrated a high energy density of 112.63 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750.83 W kg−1. This breakthrough establishes AGQDs as a leading contender for high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems, opening up new prospects for sustainable and efficient energy solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) have gained significant attention for their extensive applications in fields such as wearable devices, smart homes, and renewable energy storage. This eco-friendly device boasts numerous advantages, including high safety levels, rapid charging and discharging rates, high power density, and exceptional cycling stability. [1–3]. Presently, there exist three prevalent electrode materials utilized in SCs: carbon materials [4], conductive polymers [5] and transition metal oxides/hydroxides [6]. Carbon-based materials offer several benefits in SCs owing to their superior conductivity, high specific surface area and environmental friendliness, making them the most widely applied electrode active materials. However, their energy density is limited by inherent charging mechanisms. In comparison, conductive polymers and transition metal oxides/hydroxides exhibit reversible Faradaic reactions, leading to better capacitive performance than carbon materials [7]. Nonetheless, conventional conductive polymers encounter challenges related to volumetric changes throughout extended charge and discharge cycles, leading to diminished cycling stability. Therefore, there is an imperative need to advance pseudocapacitive materials, specifically those derived from transition metal oxides/hydroxides, aiming to optimize the efficacy of energy storage and conversion devices.

To date, researchers have extensively explored various electrode materials to boost the efficacy of SC. Among different transition metal hydroxides [8], layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have gained significant attention [9,10] because of their high theoretical capacitance [11], high specific surface area [12], excellent catalytic activity [13], rich redox reactions [14] and exceptional ion exchange capabilities [15]. The commonly used formula
M
2 +
1 − x
M
3 +
x
OH
2
x +
A
n −
x / n
· m
H 2
O describes LDHs.
M
2 +
and
M
3 +
represent two-valent and three-valent cations, respectively, while
A
n −
can be any charge-compensating anion. x represents the metal ratio
M
3 +
/
M
2 +
+
M
3 +
, and m represents the amount of crystalline water in the interlayer region. Ni and Co have different oxidation states within the hydroxide, allowing them to provide multiple charge storage and release locations. Moreover, LDH possesses a layered structure where metal ions exist as hydroxides between the layers. This structure gives the material a high surface area, providing abundant active sites for electrochemical reactions. Particularly, nickel-cobalt layered double hydroxide (NiCo-LDH) is considered a favorable electrode material for SC due to its exceptionally high theoretical capacitance at 3000 F g−1 and excellent redox activity. However, the poor conductivity of LDHs significantly limits the application of electrode modifications using LDHs [16]. To address this challenge, an efficient approach involves integrating highly conductive carbon materials. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) emerge as novel zero-dimensional carbon entities, characterized by dimensions smaller than 10 nm. They exhibit excellent application prospects and development potential in areas such as bioimaging [17], electrochemical biosensors [18] and biomedicine [19], due to their biocompatibility [20], wide applicability [21], high stability [22] and environmental friendliness [23]. GQDs possess functional groups on their edges, which result in high solubility in different solvents. Consequently, the prepared nanocomposites have shown improved electrochemical properties, significantly enhancing electron transfer rate, electrolyte contact area and conductivity. The synergistic effect between GQDs and other nanomaterials, such as LDHs, holds great potential and advantages in emerging fields like high-performance electrocatalysis and electrochemical devices [24]. Yuan et al. [25] mixed 2D nickel-cobalt LDHs with 0D GQDs to prepare a high-performance GQDs/NiCo-LDH electrode material exhibiting a specific capacity of 2220 F g−1 (at 1 A g−1). All-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitors (ASC) GQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC had an energy density reaching up to 50.84 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 8 kW kg−1 and superior flexibility. However, current methods for GQDs synthesis suffer from small-scale production, non-uniform particle size and low yield, leading to decreased SC performance due to GQDs aggregation.

In this study, we prepared AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode material by introducing asphalt-based graphene quantum dots (AGQDs) onto the surface of NiCo-LDH through a hydrothermal method. The amalgamation of AGQDs and NiCo-LDH gave rise to a cooperative influence, leading to improved conductivity of LDHs and enhanced electrochemical performance. Experimental results showed that the composite material exhibited the optimal electrochemical properties when the AGQDs solution was 20 ml. The specific capacitance of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 was 1671.27 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, and after 5000 cycles at 10 A g−1, the retention rate was 75.94 %, demonstrating commendable cycling stability for the composite electrode. The assembled AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//activated carbon (AC) ASC provided a capacity of 500.19 F g−1 and demonstrated a long cycle life (retention rate of 71.0 % after 5000 cycles). Furthermore, the assembled AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC all-solid-state ASC exhibited an energy density of 112.63 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750.83 W kg−1. It retained a substantial energy density of 79.38 Wh kg−1, even under a high power density of 7500.24 W kg−1, with a long-term stability of 72.0 % after 2000 cycles. The study suggests that AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC exhibits significant promise for practical applications in high-performance all-solid-state ASCs.

Synthesis of AGQDs: 200 mg of coal tar pitch was dispersed in 200 ml of hydrogen peroxide and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 2 h. The mixture was subjected to reflux at 100 °C for a duration of 2 h in a round bottom flask. After this period, a noticeable color transformation occurred in the solution, shifting from a dark brown hue to a dark yellow shade, indicating the successful formation of AGQDS. The solution was then allowed to cool to ambient temperature and filtered to eliminate any larger insoluble particles. This filtration process resulted in the obtainment of the AGQDs solution.

Synthesis of NiCo-LDH: 1.745 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (6 mmol), 0.873 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (3 mmol) and 2.4 g of CH4N2O (40 mmol) were dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water and vigorously stirred for 1 h at room temperature. The resulting blend was subsequently moved to a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave coated with polytetrafluoroethylene and subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the product was washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol, followed by overnight drying at 60 °C to obtain the NiCo-LDH-120-20.

Synthesis of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-x-y: 1.745 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (6 mmol), 0.873 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (3 mmol) and 2.4 g of CH4N2O (40 mmol) were dissolved in 30 ml of distilled water and stirred vigorously for 1 h. Then, 20 ml of AGQDs solution was added and stirred for 30 min. The resulting blend was then transferred to a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h. Following cooling to ambient temperature, the resultant product underwent multiple washes with deionized water and ethanol before being dried overnight at 60 °C to yield AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20. The other comparison samples prepared are named AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-x-y. Here, x represents adjusting the hydrothermal temperature (100–140 °C), and y represents the added AGQDs solution (10-30 ml).

A blend of activated carbon, carbon black, and PTFE was mixed in a mass ratio of 8:1:1 to create a slurry. The slurry was then coated onto a nickel foam substrate measuring 10 × 10 mm2 and subjected to overnight drying at 80 °C. This composite material was designated as AC. In parallel, the synthesis of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 was carried out on a nickel foam substrate of the same dimensions (10 × 10 mm2).

The preparation process for the PVA-KOH gel electrolyte involved introducing 10 g of PVA powder into 100 mL of a 3 M KOH solution. Following a 3-hour stirring period at 90 °C, the PVA-KOH gel electrolyte was successfully synthesized.

The AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 material, functioning as the positive electrode, and the AC material, serving as the negative electrode, were immersed in a PVA-KOH gel electrolyte for a duration of 30 min. Subsequently, the two electrodes were pressed together to create an all-solid-state ASC known as AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC.

The examination of sample morphology and microstructure involved utilizing a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, model JEOL JSM-7100F) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, model JEM-2100F). The analysis of structure and chemical composition was carried out using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained using an ASAP 2020 analyzer.

The electrochemical behavior was analyzed employing both an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) and a LAND test system (CT2001A). A three-electrode configuration was utilized, with the working electrode comprising the as-prepared samples, a platinum foil acting as the counter electrode, and an Hg/HgO reference electrode in use. The specific capacitance (C, measured in F g−1) was ascertained by scrutinizing galvanostatic discharge curves at various current densities, as outlined by Eq. (1):
(1)
C =
I × ∆ t
m × ∆ V
Here, I represents the discharge current (A), m is the active material mass loading (g), ∆ t is the discharge time (s) and ∆ V is the potential window (V).

In order to maintain charge balance between the positive and negative electrodes of the ASC, the mass of electroactive materials on each electrode was computed using the equality
Q +
=
Q −
, as per Eq. (2):
(2)
M +
M −
=
C −
× ∆
V −
C +
× ∆
V +

In this context, where m represents the mass loading (g), C signifies the specific capacitance (F g−1), and ∆ V represents the potential window (V), the energy density E (Wh kg−1) and power density P (W kg−1) of the ASC were calculated using formulae (3) and (4). It is noteworthy that these calculations provide crucial insights into the performance characteristics of the ASC, aiding in the comprehensive evaluation of its energy storage capabilities.
(3)
E =
1 2
C × ∆
V 2
(4)
P =
E
∆ t
where ∆ V (V) represents the potential and ∆ t (h) denotes the discharge time.

In impedance fitting, Rct represents charge transfer resistance, which reflects the resistance of electron or ion transport at the electrochemical interface. Rs represents the solution resistance, which reflects the resistance of the electrolyte solution to current transmission. CEP represents electrode surface capacitance, which is related to the charge bilayer formed at the electrochemical interface, reflecting the ability of electrode surface charge separation and accumulation.

The preparation procedure for AGQDs/NiCo-LDH is depicted in Fig. 1 The prepared AGQDs solution was transferred into a lined polytetrafluoroethylene container together with a homogeneous solution containing Co2+, Ni3+ and urea. Following that, the blend underwent a hydrothermal treatment lasting 6 h at a temperature of 120 °C. After washing and drying, it resulted in sea urchin-like microspheres, which were used as electrode material for SC.

Fig. 2a show the SEM images of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 sample, revealing the presence of sea urchin-like microspheres with a diameter of approximately 6 μm. The results in Fig. S1 demonstrate the promoting effect of elevated reaction temperature on spike growth. Under the influence of spiky structures, the microspheres are better dispersed, effectively preventing aggregation among them. The dispersion induced by this spike, along with the porous structure, contributes to a substantial active surface area. Figs. 2b and S2 show the TEM images of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 sample and other comparison samples, clearly showing that AGQDs are uniformly attached to the peaks, which helps to reduce the resistance of electrons and increase their mobility, thereby enhancing the electron transfer characteristics of the material. Fig. 2c shows the uniform distribution of AGQDs, indicating the successful preparation of AGQDs. Upon close examination of the HRTEM image in Fig. 2d, we can observe clear and well-defined lattice fringes. These fringes exhibit varying interplanar spacing, measuring approximately 0.256, 0.227, 0.193 and 0.148 nm. These distinct spacings correspond to the crystallographic planes of NiCo-LDH, namely the (012), (015), (018) and (113) planes, respectively. The SAED pattern clearly indicates the polycrystalline nature of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20. Additionally, the HAADF image depicted in Fig. 2e showcases the uniform distribution of Ni, Co, C and O elements. These findings collectively support the conclusion that the desired sea urchin-like microspheres of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 were successfully synthesized.

XRD is currently being used to investigate the crystal structures of several composite materials, including NiCo-LDH-120, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-100-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-140-20.

Fig. 3a exhibits distinct peaks at 2θ = 34.9°, 39.5°, 46.9°and 62.3°, which can be attributed to the (012), (015), (018) and (113) crystallographic planes of NiCo-LDH(PDF#33-0429), respectively. Confirming the presence of NiCo-LDH in the sample [26]. The peak of GQDs contribution is located at 26.5°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon (PDF#65-6212). Compared to the original NiCo-LDH-120, the composite materials AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30 exhibit slightly enhanced peak intensity and shift in the diffraction peaks of the crystal structure. In terms of the sample temperature, the diffraction peaks of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-100-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-140 have weakened slightly compared to AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20.

Based on the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) theory, we obtain N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of six samples of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH sea-urchin-like microspheres (Fig. 3b) and analyze them. The findings suggest that the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms display a typical shape of IV isotherms and demonstrate an H3-type hysteresis loop (P/P0 > 0.4) [27], which indicates the presence of mesopores. In addition, we study the pore size distribution using the BJH method (Fig. 3c). All six samples show a wide pore size distribution, indicating that a porous electrode material is synthesized. According to the data presented in Table S1, the incorporation of AGQDs at a hydrothermal temperature of 120 °C results in a notable enhancement of the specific surface area. Specifically, the specific surface area of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 is measured to be 149.05 m2 g−1, slightly higher than that of NiCo-LDH-120 (135.83 m2 g−1). The formation of a porous structure in NiCo-LDH can be attributed to the incorporation of AGQDs. This addition promotes the creation of mesopores, leading to an increase in the specific surface area and average pore size of the material. The pore volume of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 (0.1771 cm3 g−1) is slightly higher than that of NiCo-LDH-120 (0.1721 cm3 g−1). Pores play a role as the electrolyte storage layer and mitigate the volume change in Faraday reaction. The average pore size of the six samples predominantly falls within the range of 4–15 nm, which further verifies the presence of mesopores. These mesopores serve as pathways for the migration of ions, facilitating efficient ion transport within the material. The larger SBET, pore volume and mesoporous structure facilitate the transfer of electrolyte ions. More active surfaces are provided for charge storage and ion exchange, thereby increasing the capacitance of the electrode material.

Figs. S3–S6 display the XPS survey spectrum, Ni 2p, Co 2p, O1s and C 1s spectra for four samples: AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30. Fig. 4 show comparative spectra of XPS for Ni 2p, Co 2p, O 1s and C 1s for the four samples of NiCo-LDH-120, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30, which are utilized to ascertain the composition and oxidation states of the elements in the products.

In Fig. 4a, the Ni 2p characteristic peaks are identified at 872.7 and 875.2 eV, which correspond to the spin-orbit splitting of Ni 2p1/2 and are in line with the orbital characteristics of Ni2+ and Ni3+ in NiCo-LDH. Furthermore, the occurrence of peaks at 855.2 and 856.9 eV for Ni 2p3/2 further suggests the coexistence of Ni2+ and Ni3+. [28]. The larger peak area for Ni2+ compared to Ni3+ suggests that Ni primarily exists in the sample in the Ni2+ valence state. Furthermore, two satellite oscillation peaks were detected at 879.6 eV and 861.4 eV in the spectra [29]. Through comparison with other spectra, it can be observed that with an increase in the number of AGQDs, the Ni 2p1/2 peak in the sample exhibits a tendency to shift towards higher binding energies. This is attributed to the interaction between AGQDs and nickel atoms, leading to changes in the electronic states surrounding the nickel atoms and resulting in the Ni 2p1/2 peak appearing at different binding energy positions.

Fig. 4b displays the Co 2p spectra, revealing peaks at 797.9 and 796.5 eV for the Co 2p1/2, which are consistent with the spin-orbit characteristics of Co2+ and Co3+ in NiCo-LDH. Additionally, the peaks at 782.5 and 780.6 eV correspond to Co 2p3/2, indicating the coexistence of Co2+ and Co3+ in the sample [30]. Furthermore, the larger area of the Co3+ peak than the Co2+ peak suggests a higher content of surface Co3+ than Co2+ [31]. The accompanying two satellite peaks are located at 803.2 and 786.1 eV, respectively. By comparing the Co 2p spectra, it can be observed that with an increasing amount of AGQDs added, the Co 2p3/2 peak in the sample tends to shift towards lower binding energy. This change is attributed to the unique chemical properties of AGQDs, which allow them to form chemical bonds with the cobalt atoms in the sample and influence the electronic properties of cobalt. This highlights the synergistic effect between AGQDs and cobalt, indicating their crucial role in controlling the properties and performance of the system.

The O 1s spectrum depicted in Fig. 4c reveals the existence of a peak at 530.4 eV (O1), indicative of an OH- group, which is likely attributed to the NiCo-LDH sample [3]. The peak observed at 531.2 eV (O2) can be ascribed to the metal-oxygen bond present in the NiCo-LDH structure, while the peak at 531.9 eV (O3) arises from absorption of oxygen molecules [32]. We can observe that with the increasing addition of AGQDs to the asphalt base, the XPS O1s peak of oxygen atoms gradually shifts towards lower binding energy. This may be because AGQDs introduce new chemical functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which can form chemical bonds with oxygen atoms in the hydroxides. At the same time, the peak value at 530.4 eV (O1) moves to a lower energy position, indicating that the AGQDs may interact with oxygen atoms and change the surface chemical properties.

The C1s XPS spectrum illustrated in Fig. 4d demonstrates the existence of three distinct carbon peaks: 283.9, 284.7 and 288.7 eV, corresponding to CC, O-C=O and CO chemical groups, respectively. The O-C=O peak is mainly derived from the interlayer anion CO3 2− of hydrotalcite [33]. Calculation and comparison indicate that with the increase of asphalt-based AGQDs, the O-C=O peak shifts to a lower energy position. This can be attributed to the redistribution of valence electrons resulting from the presence of AGQDs on the surface of layered double hydroxides. In summary, AGQDs increase the electron transfer rate of NiCo-LDH, reduce the charge transfer resistance, and also provide a larger active reaction area.

The electrochemical properties of the prepared electrode materials were investigated using a three-electrode system. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the NiCo-LDH-120, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-100-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-140-20 electrodes in the potential range of 0–0.65 V at 5 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 5a. It is evident that the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode has the highest electrochemical capacity, as indicated by its significantly larger CV curve integral area compared to the other electrodes [34]. The observation of a pair of redox peaks in each CV curve reveals that the prepared electrode exhibits reversible redox reactions, thereby demonstrating pseudocapacitive behavior. The specific capacitance properties of the electrode are primarily governed by the faradaic mechanisms associated with the conversion of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+. The incorporation of AGQDs into the composite material leads to a reduction in the magnitude of the potential difference observed between the redox peaks, indicating better reversibility after the incorporation of AGQDs. [35]

Fig. 5b shows the Galvanostatic Charge-Discharge (GCD) curves of the different electrodes at a current density of 1 A g−1. Remarkably, the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode showcases superior performance with the highest specific capacitance and prolonged discharge time compared to other samples. This is mainly because the introduction of AGQDs solution improves the electron transfer rate, charging/discharging efficiency, and capacity. NiCo-LDH has abundant active sites, providing more reaction interfaces for charge storage/release. The high-temperature synthesis process improves the crystallinity of the material and generates ion transport channels, further optimizing the activity and charge transfer efficiency in charge discharge reactions, thereby achieving high capacity energy storage capacity.

Fig. 5c illustrates the CV curves of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode at various scan rates ranging from 1 to 20 mV s−1. With increasing scan rate, the overall shape of the curve remains relatively unchanged; however, the oxidation peak shifts towards a higher potential, while the reduction peak shifts towards a lower potential. This observation highlights the exceptional rate performance of the electrode, indicating efficient electron and ion transfer and diffusion during the Faraday redox process. In Fig. S7, the CV curves of the other electrodes at different scan rates are displayed, and the characteristic redox peaks, indicative of pseudocapacitive behavior, can be attributed to the following reactions involving Ni and Co ions [36]:
Ni
OH
2
+ O
H −
↔ NiOOH +
H 2
O +
e −
Co
OH
2
+ O
H −
↔ CoOOH +
H 2
O +
e −
CoOOH + O
H −
↔ Co
O 2
+
H 2
O +
e −

Figs. 5d and S7 are the GCD curves of all samples. The specific capacitances of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 at 1, 2, 5, 7 and 10 A g−1 are 1671.27, 1569.09, 1531.82, 1442 and 1352.73 F g−1, respectively. It is evident that the composite electrode of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 demonstrates a remarkable rate capability, as it exhibits an 80.9 % retention of high capacity under a tenfold increase in current density relative to the initial specific capacitance. The remarkable performance of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 can be ascribed to its distinctive sea urchin-like mesoporous structure and substantial specific surface area, which facilitate the transmission of electrolytes and enable rapid interaction between the active sites of the composite electrode and electrolyte species, thereby promoting better charge transfer kinetics. Additionally, the incorporation of AGQDs enhances conductivity and accelerates electron transfer [37,38].

In Fig. 5e, the specific capacitances of NiCo-LDH-120, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-10, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-30, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-100-20 and AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-140-20 electrodes at 1 A g−1 are 943.5, 847.1, 1671.3, 1369.5, 1296.5 and 1168.7 F g−1, respectively. It is evident that with the increasing current density within the range of 1 to 10 A g−1, the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite material consistently maintains a higher specific capacitance compared to the other materials. This phenomenon can be attributed to the synergistic interplay between metals and GQDs, resulting in enhanced performance. Table 1 highlights the superior specific capacitance of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 compared to other reported compounds.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results measured in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz are shown in Fig. 5f, where all curves are composed of a high-frequency semicircle and a low-frequency sloping line. In the high-frequency region, the X-axis intercept of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode curve representing the equivalent series resistance (Rs) is lower than that of other materials, demonstrating reduced internal resistance and the capacity to significantly enhance the electrochemical performance of the SC [45]. In addition, the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode material has the smallest diameter in the semicircle region, indicating its lower charge transfer resistance at the interface and better conductivity than other electrodes [46]. This may be due to the multi-level structure of the composite material providing a suitable structure for electron and ion transport. In the low-frequency region, the slope of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 is the largest, indicating a faster electrolyte diffusion and ion transport rate. AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 has abundant mesopores and an expansive specific surface area, which can maintain good capacitance at high current densities. Both the EIS and GCD curves indicate that the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode exhibits a favorable ion transfer rate, which is indicative of its outstanding performance. The cycling stability test of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 composite electrode at a current density of 10 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 5g. This result demonstrates the commendable cycle stability of the composite electrode material, as indicated by the significant capacity retention rate of 75.94 % after 5000 cycles of GCD.

To further assess the efficacy of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH electrodes in SC systems, AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC ASC was prepared using AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20, activated carbon (AC) and 6 mol KOH. The specific capacitance of AC is measured to be 110 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Therefore, according to Eq. (2), the optimal mass loading ratio of the active material is calculated to be
m +
/
m −
= 0.116 . Consequently, the masses of the positive electrode and negative electrode are determined to be 2.2 × 10−3 g and 1.807 × 10−2 g, respectively.

Fig. 6a shows the CV curves of the cathode and anode. In Fig. 6b, the working potential window of the ASC can be extended to 1.6 V. The CV curves of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC at different scan rates are nearly identical (Fig. 6c), indicating fast ion and electron transfer speeds [8]. They both exhibit distinct redox peaks, demonstrating pseudocapacitive behavior in the ASC. Fig. 6d shows the GCD curves at different current densities. The specific capacitance of the ASC is 500.19, 491.13, 476.56, 453.25 and 416.88 F g−1 at different current densities. Fig. S8 also proves that AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 has better performance. After 5000 cycles, the capacity retention rate is 71 %, as shown in Fig. 6e.

The all-solid-state ASC was assembled by utilizing AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 as the positive electrode, AC as the negative electrode and PVA-KOH as the electrolyte, in order to further assess the practical application of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH-120-20 (Fig. 7a). In Fig. 7b, the operational potential window of the ASC device extends to 1.6 V. Furthermore, with increasing scan rates, the CV curve retains a consistent shape, indicating the outstanding rate capability of the ASC device (Fig. 7c). To further assess the rate performance intuitively, GCD curves were measured at different current densities (Fig. 7d). It is noteworthy that the ASC device retains 70.4 % of its capacitance when the current density increases from 1 to 10 A g−1. Moreover, the ASC device exhibits high specific capacitances of 360.4, 346.67, 307.67, 273 and 254 F g−1 at 1, 2, 5, 7 and 10 A g−1 (Fig. 7e), respectively. The obtained Ragone plot of the ASC, as well as previously reported ASCs, is shown in Fig. 7f. The AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC exhibits a high energy density of 112.63 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750.83 W kg−1. It is worth noting that even at a high power density of 7500.24 W kg−1, the energy density remains at 79.38 Wh kg−1. This performance is nearly superior to previously reported LDH electrode materials, such as CoSx/NiCo-LDH//AC (35.8 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1) [43], (Ni,Co)Se2/NiCo-LDH//PC (39 Wh kg−1 at 1650 W kg−1) [44], GQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC (50.84 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 802.8 W kg−1) [47], NiCoAlLDH/NF//PAC (41.44 Wh kg−1 at 799.9 W kg−1) [48], PANI/LDH-200//AC (46 Wh kg−1 at 351.6 W kg−1) [49], NiCoAl-LDH/NRG//AC (13.1 Wh kg−1 at 1637.5 W kg−1) [50], MXene/CoAl-LDH//MG (30.9 Wh kg−1 at 992.6 W kg−1) [51], CoAl-LDH//AG (16.275 Wh kg−1 at 3500 W kg−1) [52] and CC@NiCo2Al-LDH//ZPC (44 Wh kg−1 at 462 W kg−1) [53]. The cyclic stability of the AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC ASC is further tested. After 5000 cycles, the capacity retention rate of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC ASC is 71 %, this is because the metal hybrid GQD material has good structural stability and is able to maintain the integrity of its original structure during multiple cycles (Fig. S10). Moreover, two series-connected all-solid-state ASC devices can illuminate a blue “TYUT” LED matrix strip, demonstrating the potential of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC as electrodes for SC (Fig. 7g).

In summary, a high-performance composite electrode material for SC was successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal method, comprising of NiCo-LDH and AGQDs. The combination of NiCo-LDH and AGQDs resulted in a synergistic effect, enhancing the conductivity and electrochemical performance of the electrode material. The resulting AGQDs/NiCo-LDH exhibited a high specific capacitance of 1671.27 F g−1, excellent rate capability and good cycling stability (75.94 %, after 5000 cycles). Furthermore, the assembled AGQDs/NiCo-LDH//AC all-solid-state ASC demonstrated a high specific capacitance of 360.4 F g−1 and a high energy density of 112.63 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750.83 W kg−1. These excellent performance metrics further underscore the promising potential of AGQDs/NiCo-LDH as a high-performance electrode material for SC. This work presents innovative approaches in electrode material design, opening up new possibilities for advancements in the field.

Yani Yan: Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lijing Xie: Supervision. Yi Zhou: Supervision. Yongfeng Li: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Cheng-Meng Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22278291), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 202203021211145), the Patent Transformation Special Plan Project of Shanxi Province (No. 202303009) and the Key Research and Development Project of Shanxi Province (Nos. 202102070301018 and 2021020660301013).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrathin Ni-Co LDH nanosheets grown on carbon fiber cloth via electrodeposition for high-performance supercapacitors
